what_it_does: This file is a Round-2 deep review and design note that answers R1-FOLD F5 by concretely designing how rule determinism can be enforced. It proposes a bounded declarative rule grammar, static and structural enforcement, per-rule readiness, a renderability model, handling for missing/pruned/failed references, and a composition escape valve for richer logic.
observations: The TL;DR claims determinism and full expressiveness do not fundamentally conflict if inputs are whitelisted and operators are pure, but renderability is the binding constraint.
Arbitrary code (sandboxed eval) is said to be both unenforceably deterministic and unrenderable, whereas a bounded pure-expression grammar can satisfy both.
The file argues that Q4's own four constraints already exclude arbitrary code, so the bounded grammar honors rather than cuts Tim's intent.
It cites observed code in nodes/gate.py, nodes/join.py, runtime/scheduler.py, address.py, and 06-rendering.md with line ranges.
It corrects the F5 phrase 'post-barrier' because scheduler.py has no global barrier; readiness is per-rule after all referenced addresses settle.
It proposes a new cognition/rules.py evaluator using an AST-not-eval approach with static whitelist-walking and a resolved-values-only eval environment.
A two-tier render model is proposed: short edge chip/label plus Inspector-expand showing the AST as a chip-tree.
It lists nine concrete adversarial cases the evaluator must reject, including time, random, order, count-so-far, model-in-rule, external state, div-by-zero, and missing references.
Missing/pruned/failed references are flagged as the key open decision: fail-loud default or optional declared on_missing, never the silent falsy fallback gate.py currently uses.
It proposes composition as the escape valve: richer logic becomes an upstream pure compute node or role, routed by a simple rule over its resolved output.
standouts: Renderability, not determinism, is identified as the hard cap on rule expressiveness.
The AST-not-eval choice is treated as the central mechanism that makes determinism enforceable rather than merely asserted.
Missing/pruned/failed references must be a declared outcome, not a silent falsy fallback.
conventions: Uses the repo epistemic tags Observed, Designed, and Open (Tim).
Marked READ-ONLY and avoids editing criteria/guides, framing itself as the design behind already-absorbed wording.
Follows a structured format: TL;DR, numbered sections, tables, adversarial cases, recommendation, evidence ledger.
References live code with file and line numbers.
concerns: Core enforcement depends on a not-yet-built cognition/rules.py module.
The existing gate.py silently treats missing/None as fail, which the document explicitly says must not be inherited by the rule evaluator.
Several critical design points (missing-reference policy, exact v1 aggregation primitives) are marked Open and require Tim confirmation before implementation.
The distinction between 'resolved' and 'settled' status for pruned/failed references is designed but not yet wired into code.
notes: A design note that turns 'asserted determinism' into an enforceable bounded rule grammar, while arguing the real ceiling is whether the rule can be drawn on the cognition canvas.
questions: What is the exact v1 operator set for the bounded grammar?
How will fail-loud rule events be emitted and recorded in the scheduler/store?
Will on_missing be permitted, and what will its syntax and semantics be?
How will the bounded AST be serialized in node/edge config?
What concrete test harness will verify replay-identity when sibling roles finish in different orders?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: it set out to design concrete enforcement for rule determinism and report where expressiveness and renderability conflict, and it does so through the AST-not-eval, readiness, renderability, and missing-reference designs.
intention_signals: It is trying to resolve F5 by replacing asserted determinism with an enforceable bounded rule grammar, preserve expressive power through composition, and keep the cognition view renderable.
novelty: It frames renderability rather than determinism as the hard constraint on how expressive rules can be, and treats missing/pruned/failed references as a declared routing outcome rather than an implicit falsy default.
contribution: A concrete enforcement design for deterministic, renderable rules: a bounded AST evaluator, whitelist/banned construct tables, static plus structural enforcement, per-rule readiness semantics, a two-tier rendering model, adversarial rejection cases, and a composition escape valve for logic beyond the grammar.
summary_for_embedding: A Round-2 design review proposing a bounded declarative AST rule evaluator that enforces determinism through static and structural checks, per-rule readiness over resolved values, two-tier renderability, and composition for richer logic.
intention_for_embedding: To answer R1-FOLD F5 by concretely designing how 'full declared logic' can be made enforceably deterministic and renderable without expanding into arbitrary code.
